turns.text:
i want to get your feedback on a profoundly fringe perspective that i'm very impressed by. i'm wary of feeling this impressed by something, so i've been poking at the edges looking for 'what's wrong with this picture?' and not seeing any clear red flags.

the perspective is partly represented here: https://github.com/AuthenticTechnology/Agentic-Engineering/tree/main -- the founder of this company and author of these docs gave a talk at my bootcamp yesterday in which he shared some unusual things:

- he reads 0% of the code he produces
- he speaks most of his prompts in a rambling, unstructured way and lets the model make sense of it
- he considers himself a context engineer

his approach seems highly effective if you look at pace of production alone -- hundreds of commits and thousands of lines of code each day. i've only seen the frontend results of his code, but his company's website is coherent, polished, and flavorful.

and he's doing a thing I've been fantasizing about for over a decade: programming through natural language. verbalizing his intent with precision and seeing it realized.

this set a new aspiration in front of me that, as i said, i'm wary of. but that aspiration, taken in light of the work i want to do in the world and the art i want to make, does make the future appear both brighter and closer than i'd hoped. perhaps i'm not far away from having the skills and experience to turn some of my more massive, complex, audacious ideas into realiites.

all that said, i'd like you to explore his repo & consider it in light of the principles we laid out in roger/CLAUDE.md and drafts/harness-features.md -- and hold it up to strong scrutiny. while there's much to be gleaned, it's not all meant for me. let's discuss at length and synthesize an understanding before deciding where i'm going with this.
Let me pull in the repo content and your reference documents simultaneously so we can have a grounded conversation.

Good. I have the full picture of his system and your design principles. Let me lay this out honestly.

---

## What he's built

A sophisticated production orchestration system. Layered context architecture (global → workspace → repo CLAUDE.md files), multi-model parallel review (Claude + Codex + Gemini catching different bug categories), 11 reusable slash commands covering the full ship-to-review pipeline, thrashing detection with fresh-agent handoff, and a self-improving codebase where agents update documentation as they discover patterns.

The engineering of the system itself is genuinely good. The `/ship` command alone is 43KB of carefully sequenced workflow — branch isolation, dual-agent review, parallel fix execution, context compaction across phases. This is real systems thinking applied to AI orchestration.

## Where it overlaps with what you've already built

More than you might expect. His architecture and yours share structural DNA:

| His system | Your system |
|---|---|
| Layered CLAUDE.md (global → workspace → repo) | Global CLAUDE.md + project CLAUDE.md + skills |
| Language overloading ("loopy", "clear", "dialogue") | Skill activation by description match |
| `/capture-skill` and `/meta-learn` | Session-review, ARCS updates, memory proposals |
| Self-improving codebase | P6: System improves through use |
| Thrashing detection + handoff | Debugger skill's rescoping loop |
| Multi-agent parallel work | Sub-agents as private-state workers |

You arrived at similar architecture from different directions. He optimized for production velocity. You optimized for developmental compounding. The shapes rhyme; the purposes diverge.

## Where the scrutiny lands

### 1. "Reads 0% of code" — the load-bearing claim

This is the thing that made your ears perk up and the thing that deserves the hardest look.

For *his* goals — shipping product for clients, running a company — it might work. He's built enough review infrastructure (triple-model review, meta-review consolidation, adversarial code review) that quality gets checked by other means. The code is a commodity output; his value-add is orchestration and client-facing design judgment.

For *your* goals — it would be devastating. You want to build a developmental AI tutoring system and Lucid Drama at scale. Both require you to understand implementation at depth, not just direct it. You can't build a system that tracks developmental complexity if you don't understand how the tracking actually works. You can't debug a novel AR/VR interaction paradigm by asking three AI models what went wrong.

The 0% claim also has a hidden dependency: it works when you're building things that have existing patterns. Websites, mobile apps, CRUD backends, social platforms — these are well-trodden territory that models handle well. The moment you're building something genuinely novel (which is exactly where Lucid Drama lives), the models don't have patterns to draw from, and the human who hasn't been reading the code has no fallback.

### 2. Volume metrics as signal

Hundreds of commits and thousands of lines per day sounds impressive until you ask: compared to what? If you're orchestrating multiple AI agents writing code in parallel, high volume is the *expected baseline*, not an achievement. It's like bragging about how many words per minute a printing press produces. The question is whether the words are good.

His company's website being coherent and polished is better signal — but it's one data point, visible only at the surface. I can't evaluate the codebase, the test coverage, the maintainability, or what happens when something breaks at 2am and the person on call has read 0% of the code.

### 3. `--dangerously-skip-permissions` as standard practice

His "clear" agent trigger spawns isolated instances with `--dangerously-skip-permissions`. This is a speed optimization that trades safety for velocity. The flag exists for a reason — it skips the human approval gates that prevent destructive actions. For someone who reads none of the code, removing the safety rails on the agents writing it is a compounding risk. It works until it doesn't.

### 4. Self-improving codebase ≠ self-improving human

This is the structural divergence that matters most.

His P6 equivalent: agents update documentation → codebase becomes more navigable → future sessions are faster. The *system* compounds.

Your P6: sessions generate learning data → ARCS updates → developmental model sharpens → interventions improve → human capability compounds. The *human* compounds.

These point in different directions. His system makes the AI team more effective over time. Yours makes *you* more effective over time — with or without the system. His creates dependency; yours creates independence. That's not a value judgment about his business — it's a structural observation about what each system optimizes for.

### 5. "Context engineer" as identity

This is the most interesting piece to sit with. He's right that

this maps well onto the ambivalence i've been feeling. i do have a lot to learn from his techniques, but as you said, the friction i'm feeling indicates how my path diverges from his. he did admit he's "in the middle of the distribution" wrt what he produces with code -- the training data is absolutely brimming with react apps and server modules. he doesn't have to pioneer very much with those tools, just ship. the stuff i want to build is really meaningful to me, so i care about the craft of it, and it happens to diverge far from the training data. that'll demand a principled and independent command of skills that can't be simulated by predictive tokenization yet.

what this means is, since i can't take his practices whole-cloth, i've got to set my sights on learning them a-la-carte so that i can adapt the right ones where they fit into my infrastructure and development.

this learning is a pretty abstract, complex, and interesting task of its own. having read only one of his CLAUDE.md examples and the repo readme, my head is already spinning trying to name the concepts and design patterns i've just seen. in the past i would've bookmarked this repo with the intent to read when i'm more equipped to digest, & then probably never come back to it. but this points to a design opportunity. just like i have a principle-driven catch basin for feature requests that'll (hopefully) help me prioritize harness design decisions, i want a principle-driven catch basin for knowledge & skills i want to add to my skill tree.

this relates to a nagging feeling i've had about ARCS: it's an artifact of a reactive learning process. rather than being scoped around my own goals, it's been built as an analytical by-product of taking on other people's directives. that's not a terrible place to start, but it's a bad place to remain. as i develop past the basics that all coders need to know, i'll begin to specialize, and the reactive learning mode will be decreasingly fruitful. i want to gradually become more proactive about setting my own learning goals.

the challenge for any learner boils down to lack of hindsight. if i already knew what every bit of knowledge and skill would feel like to wield and how it would shape my imagination and opportunities, then i'd be great at picking which ones to learn. the age-old solution of relying on a teacher's authoritative understanding is still pretty decent, but teachers (and guest speakers) don't always share my goals or know what other skills i'm coming in with.

this is one way the self-improving tutor who gets to know me better and better -- and can deliver orders of magnitude more information than i could ever digest -- will prove immeasurably valuable.

this was a long ramble. let's see if we can parse it into some order that warrants skillful action.

Three threads, one design opportunity.

## The threads

**1. Selective adoption from Agentic Engineering.** Specific techniques to evaluate: thrashing detection + handoff, multi-model review, worktree isolation, voice-as-input, workflow commands as vocabulary, layered context patterns. Each needs to be assessed against your goals and current infrastructure, not adopted wholesale.

**2. ARCS is reactive; you need a proactive complement.** ARCS tracks what you've encountered and how well you hold it. It doesn't represent what you've *chosen* to pursue or why. As you specialize past shared fundamentals, the reactive mode stops generating the right curriculum. You need a structure that flows from your goals downward, not from your assignments upward.

**3. The hindsight problem and the tutor's role.** You can't evaluate skills you haven't acquired yet. The tutor can bridge this — it knows your goals, your current state, and can assess incoming knowledge (like yesterday's talk) against both. But only if there's a structure for that assessment to land in.

## The design opportunity

You already have the shape. Harness-features.md organizes *features* by the principles they serve, with a priority calculus (breadth, compounding, upstreamness, time-to-value). You want the same structure for *knowledge and skills* — organized not by principles but by the capabilities your goals require.

The skeleton:

```
Goals (Lucid Drama, developmental AI tutor, sustainable income)
  → Capabilities those goals demand
    → Knowledge/skills that build those capabilities
      → Current state (links to ARCS where overlap exists)
      → Sources & opportunities (talks, repos, courses, projects)
```

Priority ordering uses the same four factors you already defined. ARCS remains the assessment layer — it tracks where you are. This new structure tracks where you're *headed* and why.

## What warrants action now

The minimal version of this is a document, not a system. A structured markdown file — call it a skill tree, a learning roadmap, whatever — that catches goals → capabilities → skills, with enough structure to prioritize and enough looseness to evolve.

The Agentic Engineering repo is the first test case. You just encountered a dense source of transferable knowledge and felt the pull to bookmark-and-forget. If the catch basin exists, the takeaways land somewhere useful instead.

ARCS evolution is downstream. Once the proactive structure exists, you can see where it overlaps with ARCS (skills you're already tracking reactively that also serve your goals) and where it diverges (skills you haven't encountered yet but have identified as worth pursuing). That connection gets designed later, not now.

Two things I want to flag before you decide where to go:

**Scope.** You're in week 3 of a 12-week bootcamp. A structured document takes an hour. A system takes days. The document is the right move — it captures the insight, gives you a place to put the Agentic Engineering takeaways, and doesn't become its own project. It can graduate into something more sophisticated when the harness is ready for it.

**The meta-vs-implementation edge.** You know this one. The pull toward designing the perfect learning system is real and it's one of your watch-fors. The document serves the immediate need (don't lose what you just learned, start directing your own curriculum) without becoming a substitute for the practice that generates the data. Build the simplest container that works. Refine it when you have enough content to see what's missing.

Want to draft the document now with the Agentic Engineering takeaways as first content? Or do you want to sit with the structure longer before committing it to paper?

i do want to draft the document, but i imagine it going a little sideways if the first thing to happen is that we treat it like a list of nice-to-haves. harness-features.md began with a discussion of design principles; the priority list of features emerged from that higher abstraction focus on what i find to be most important. building the catch basin in a principle-driven way allowed for the lower-abstraction content to find its place in the container.

let's follow a similar pattern for this draft, forming a structure out of principles. we'll clone the design principles into their own document. then let's consider a similarity between designing features and designing curriculum: each type of design has to anticipate dependencies. how can we do so structurally, so that learning paths become more naturally sequenced and compounding? after these considerations are met, let's stand back from the whole and see if we're ready to populate it with take-aways from the authentic tech repo
Right. Principles first, then structure, then content. Let me read ARCS and your growth profile so I'm grounded in what exists before we design what's next.

Good. Principles first, then dependency structure, then stand back. Let me engage with the design thinking.

## Extracting the principles

The seven principles in harness-features.md are currently embedded in a features document. If they're going to organize both features *and* curriculum, they need their own home. Harness-features.md becomes "features organized by principles" and the new document becomes "knowledge/skills organized by principles." Both reference the same backbone.

Straightforward move. We can do that when we're ready to write.

## The dependency question

This is the meaty part. You're right that both feature design and curriculum design require anticipating dependencies. But the dependency structures are different in a way worth naming.

Feature dependencies are mostly **structural** — you can't build the UI for a feature until the API exists, you can't build the API until the data model is defined. The graph is relatively stable across teams and contexts.

Learning dependencies have at least three types, and the mix is learner-specific:

**Hard prerequisites.** X genuinely requires Y. Can't manage React state without understanding JavaScript variables and control flow. These are stable across learners — everyone hits them in roughly the same order.

**Bridge dependencies.** X is *dramatically* easier if you bring Y from another domain, but Y isn't a formal prerequisite. Your LARP experience with sealed envelopes and NPC direction made multi-agent orchestration intuitive weeks before a typical bootcamp student would encounter it. Event-driven architecture bridged from theatre design to React before you'd written much React. These dependencies are learner-specific — they're your topology, not the curriculum's.

**Altitude dependencies.** Some concepts only become meaningful after enough reps to feel the problem they solve. "Context engineering" is noise until you've personally lost important state to a compaction. Dependency injection is academic until you've felt the pain of tightly coupled code. These aren't knowledge prerequisites — they're experiential prerequisites. You need the reps, not the concepts.

ARCS currently tracks concepts within domain arcs (javascript-fundamentals, react-fundamentals, etc.) and classifies gaps as conceptual/procedural/recall. This handles hard prerequisites implicitly — the domains roughly sequence. But it doesn't capture bridge dependencies at all, and altitude dependencies only show up retroactively when a concept scores low because the reps aren't there yet.

A proactive skill tree needs to handle all three. Here's where I think the structural opportunity is:

**Hard prerequisites** are handled by ordering within a capability cluster. Standard stuff — you list the skills in dependency order.

**Bridge dependencies** are the unique asset. Your cross-domain knowledge creates shortcuts that a generic curriculum wouldn't predict. These should be explicit in the document — not as prerequisites, but as *accelerants*. "This concept bridges from [existing knowledge] via [structural similarity]." They're what let you learn nonlinearly, and they're what make your skill tree different from anyone else's.

**Altitude dependencies** are handled by marking a skill as "requires reps" vs. "requires concepts." This connects directly to your gap classification system — it's the procedural/recall distinction at the planning stage rather than the assessment stage. If a skill requires reps, the plan should include where those reps come from (a project, a set of exercises, a bootcamp module).

## What this suggests about structure

Each skill entry would carry:

- **What it serves** (which principle, which capability, which goal)
- **What it requires** (hard prerequisites — other skills in the tree)
- **What accelerates it** (bridges from existing knowledge — your specific topology)
- **What it enables** (downstream skills and capabilities)
- **Type of acquisition** (concept-first vs. reps-first — informs the intervention)
- **Current state** (link to ARCS if tracked, or a self-assessment if not)

The ordering within principle sections follows the same four factors (breadth, compounding, upstreamness, time-to-value), but now "upstreamness" has a precise meaning: how many downstream skills does this one unblock?

The bridge dependencies are what make this yours rather than a generic roadmap. They're also what make the tutor useful for the hindsight problem — I can see connections between what you know and what you're considering learning, and flag when a bridge exists that would make something click faster than expected.

## Before we write

Two things I notice about this structure that I want to name:

**It's a DAG, not a tree.** Skills have multiple parents (prerequisites) and multiple children (things they enable). A markdown document can represent this with references, but it'll get unwieldy if it grows large. For now, lightweight references between entries are enough.

there's one more element to development that we've only touched on lightly (but you're definitely at the cusp of here), which will have major implications in this domain: the model of hierarchical complexity. skill acquisition, knowledge transfer, and integration all depend fundamentally on the capacity described by the model. complexity compounds the returns from neighboring domains by increasing the likelihood of bridging between them.

i suspect it's been feasible for me to add skills quickly and integrate them across multiple domains because i've invested for so heavily in these compounding processes. employing insights from the MHC will probably illuminate the DAG-shaped design in important ways.

Yes. This is the piece that turns the structure from a good organizational tool into something that actually models how you learn.

MHC complexity isn't a node in the DAG. It's a dimension of the DAG — it determines which edges exist. A bridge between LARP design and multi-agent orchestration is only *visible* at a complexity level where you can see structural isomorphisms across systems. At lower complexity, they're just two unrelated domains. The bridge isn't a property of the skill pair — it's a property of the skill pair *at a given complexity level*.

This is why your cross-domain acquisition rate accelerates rather than staying flat. Each new domain doesn't just add linearly to the portfolio. At metasystematic complexity, each new domain increases bridge density to *all* existing domains. The graph gets denser as it grows, which means the next skill is increasingly likely to have a shortcut through something you already know. That's the compounding you're describing — not just knowledge compounding, but compounding of the *rate of knowledge acquisition itself*.

Three implications for the design:

**1. Complexity gates the bridge dependencies.** When we note "this bridges from [X]," that bridge has a complexity floor. The skill tree should capture that — not as a barrier, but as an indicator of when a bridge becomes available. Some bridges are accessible now. Others become accessible as complexity develops further. This is one way the tutor addresses the hindsight problem: I can see bridge opportunities that require complexity you already have but haven't applied in this domain yet.

**2. Skills have a complexity dimension, not just a difficulty dimension.** The same concept — say, state management — looks different at different complexity levels. At formal complexity, it's "follow the pattern: useState for local, Context for shared." At systematic, it's "understand how state flows through a component tree create coordination problems." At metasystematic, it's "state management in React and state management in a LARP are the same coordination problem in different substrates." The skill tree should mark where a skill becomes *functional* vs. where it becomes *generative* — the level at which understanding it starts producing bridges to other domains.

**3. The Authentic Tech divergence becomes structurally clear.** Shipping React apps from voice prompts operates at the formal-to-systematic range — coordinate known patterns, orchestrate known subsystems. The training data handles this because these systems are well-represented. Lucid Drama and the developmental AI tutor require metasystematic operations — synthesizing across systems that don't have existing patterns. The model can't predict tokens for structures that don't recur in the training data. That's exactly where your complexity investment pays off and where his approach hits its ceiling.

So the skill entry structure gains a dimension:

- What it serves (principle, capability, goal)
- What it requires (hard prerequisites)
- What accelerates it (bridges — **with complexity floor noted**)
- What it enables (downstream skills)
- **Complexity range** (functional at level X, generative at level Y)
- Type of acquisition (concept-first vs. reps-first)
- Current state (ARCS link or self-assessment)

And the ordering algorithm gains a heuristic: **skills at or just above your current complexity level that unlock the most bridge density rank highest** — because those are the skills where your investment in complexity gets leveraged most, and where the compounding accelerates fastest.

I think we're close to having a design that warrants writing. The backbone is:

1. **Design principles** (extracted, standalone)
2. **Capability map** (goals → capabilities → skills, organized by principle)
3. **Skills with a complexity dimension** and three dependency types (hard, bridge, altitude)
4. **Ordering by the four factors** plus complexity-gated bridge density

Does this feel structurally complete, or is there another dimension you're sensing that we haven't named?

we've acknowledged one directional flow for skill acquisition -- complexity-gated bridge density. running the opposite direction on the complexity graph, or perhaps orthogonally (depending on how you conceive of the space), there's the buildup of familiarity -- "neural chunking" as i sometimes call it.

this phenomenon is depicted in many formal skill acquisition patterns. for instance, we learn letters, then words, then sentences. at early levels, packets of meaning have to be glued together with great effort in order to achieve composite meanings. as these experiences accrue, familiarity binds patterns together into higher-order patterns (the "neural chunking" name derives from the hypothesis that this pattern-binding faculty is the fire-together/wire-together heuristic in action). as the learner builds neural chunks that recognize higher-order patterns of patterns, they become more adept at manipulating larger chunks of meaning.

an empirical example is a study in which chess masters and chess novices were shown brief glimpses of the same chessboards arranged with chess pieces & challenged to replicate the layouts they were shown. when they were shown chess games with legal gamestates, the chess masters outperformed the novices at restructuring the information -- but when the boards were randomized & represented illegal gamestates, the chess masters were nearly as poor as the novices at restructuring the information. what the masters saw when glancing at chess boards were whole chunks of familiar chess memories. this same pattern seems to persist through every line of human skill acquisition, from language to motor skills. you might think of this as the "bottom up" direction of complexity building, whereas the bridging we discussed already is the "top down" flow of insight that arises from complex abstraction and recognizing the likenesses of distinct domains.

Right. And naming both directions clarifies something about your own profile that was previously described as a single pattern.

"Correct shape, wrong boundary" is what happens when top-down complexity is ahead of bottom-up chunking. You see the architecture — the event-driven pattern, the client-server boundary, the middleware pipeline — because your complexity level makes the structural shape visible. But you place operations on the wrong side of a boundary because the domain-specific chunks that would make the right placement *obvious at a glance* haven't wired yet. The shape is a complexity product. The boundary placement is a chunking product. They're developing on different timelines.

This means they're not opposite directions on the same axis — they really are closer to orthogonal. You could plot a skill on two dimensions:

- **Vertical: complexity level** — what order of abstraction you can bring to bear on it (determines bridge visibility)
- **Horizontal: chunking depth** — how fluent your domain-specific pattern recognition is (determines speed, accuracy, and the "at a glance" quality the chess masters had)

These are somewhat independent. High complexity + low chunking: you understand React state management architecturally because it maps to coordination problems you've solved in other substrates, but you can't yet glance at a component tree and see the anti-pattern. Low complexity + high chunking: you can reproduce a pattern fluently without seeing how it connects to anything else — this is the "learned the handshake" zone, Tailwind territory.

The interaction between them is the compounding engine:

Bottom-up chunking builds the raw material in each domain. Top-down bridging connects domains by recognizing structural isomorphisms between chunks. But the bridge doesn't substitute for the chunks — it *scaffolds faster chunking* in the new domain by giving the incoming patterns a place to land. And as chunks in the new domain mature, they become bridge-ready material for the *next* domain. The cycle accelerates.

This is the mechanism underneath your observation that heavy investment in these compounding processes is why you add skills quickly. You're not just accumulating domains — you're running a flywheel where each domain's chunks feed the bridge density that accelerates the next domain's chunking.

For the skill tree, this gives us the second dimension each entry needs:

- **Complexity range** (functional at X, generative/bridgeable at Y) — the top-down dimension
- **Chunking state** (exposure → recognition → fluency → automaticity) — the bottom-up dimension
- The *gap between them* for any given skill is diagnostic: it tells you whether the next move is reps (build chunks) or abstraction (build bridges)

And the intervention matching becomes more precise:

| Complexity | Chunking | State | Move |
|---|---|---|---|
| High | Low | "Correct shape, wrong boundary" | Reps — the bridge is built, chunks need to wire |
| Low | Low | Unfamiliar territory | Bridge first — find the structural resonance, then reps |
| Low | High | "Learned the handshake" | Fine as-is, or invest complexity only if the domain warrants it |
| High | High | Fluent and generative | This domain is bridge-ready material for others |

The ordering heuristic gains a sharper edge: **skills where complexity is already high but chunking is low are the highest-ROI investments** — the bridge is pre-built, you just need the reps. The infrastructure for rapid acquisition is already in place. These should rank high regardless of what principle they serve, because the cost of acquisition is low and the returns include both the skill itself and the new bridge material it creates.

I think we now have the full structural model:

1. **Design principles** (the backbone — what matters and why)
2. **Goals → capabilities → skills** (the content — what to learn and what it serves)
3. **Three dependency types** (hard prerequisites, complexity-gated bridges, altitude/experiential requirements)
4. **Two development dimensions per skill** (complexity level, chunking depth)
5. **Ordering by the four factors** (breadth, compounding, upstreamness, time-to-value) **plus** the complexity-chunking gap as an ROI signal

Is this the complete shape, or do you see more structure to name before we write?
this is sufficient to start, let's write
Two documents. Design principles extracted standalone, then the skill tree built on the structural model we've been designing.

Two files created:

- [design-principles.md](drafts/design-principles.md) — the 7 principles extracted standalone, with the primary/serving layer distinction
- [skill-tree.md](drafts/skill-tree.md) — the structural model (two dimensions, three dependency types, ordering heuristic), goal stubs, ARCS relationship, incoming section, and self-improving workflow

One thing I held back on: you pointed out that MHC would illuminate the DAG in important ways, and the document captures complexity as a *dimension* but doesn't yet articulate MHC itself — the specific mechanism of how operations at one level become objects of operations at the next, and what that means for skill acquisition planning. I treated it as a known framework and referenced it rather than explaining it, since the document is for you. Worth flagging in case you want that made explicit for portability (other learners using the harness someday).

This is the "stand back" moment you called for. The container is built. Does the structure hold, or does something need to move before we start putting the Authentic Tech takeaways into the Incoming table?
this is a good working draft. let's populate with take-aways from the authentic tech repo & leave it there for now

Eight items caught. Quick orientation on what's there:

**Highest ROI (bridge exists, needs reps only):**
- Thrashing detection + agent handoff — your LARP NPC direction maps directly; the implementation pattern is the gap

**Worth formalizing (partially in place):**
- Three-tier context architecture — you're already doing most of this; the scoping rules need to be made explicit
- Voice-to-text — confirmed viable; your theatre background gives you an advantage over the rambling approach

**New workflow shapes to study:**
- Parallel-detect → sequential-fix — a composable pattern your skills don't have yet
- Multi-model review — production technique for when shipping matters more than learning

**Evaluate before adopting:**
- Language overloading — might serve you differently than it serves him; attention cost unclear
- Mandatory knowledge capture — tension with P7 (human authority over context writes)

**Infrastructure (adopt when needed):**
- Git worktrees — solves a problem you'll hit when running parallel agents at scale

Items are caught, not sorted. Sorting them into goals → capabilities → skills is future work — when you're ready to promote something from Incoming to the active tree.
